subject: Astronomy & Space
title: Supernova Remnants
description: A quick, practical guide to Supernova Remnants.

hook.text: After a supernova explodes, the expanding shell of gas creates a glowing remnant that can last thousands of years.

buildup.text: The blast wave slams into surrounding gas at thousands of km/s, heating it to millions of degrees and making it glow in X-rays.

discovery.text: The Crab Nebula is a famous remnant. At its center spins a pulsar—a neutron star flashing 30 times per second.

twist.text: Remnants are cosmic recyclers. They seed space with heavy elements that get swept into new stars and planets.

climax.text: Cassiopeia A is the youngest known remnant in the Milky Way—about 340 years old and still brilliantly glowing.

punchline.text: Glowing shells of star debris. Recycling elements into the next generation of worlds.

quiz.question: What role do supernova remnants play in the cosmos?
options: They seed space with heavy elements that become part of new stars and planets
They collapse to form new black holes at their centers
They block light from distant galaxies, creating cosmic shadows
